meta.title: Grammar
meta.subtitle: Gender, articles, verbs, tenses, negation, questions, pronouns, and register — with English and Russian comparison notes where they help.

blocks.text: All French nouns are either masculine or feminine. There is no neuter.

🇷🇺 Good news: You already handle grammatical gender in Russian — and Russian has three genders. French is actually simpler: just two. 🇬🇧 English has no grammatical gender, so this is a new concept for English-only speakers.
Patterns to Guess Gender
These are tendencies, not ironclad rules:
Usually Masculine:

items: Nouns ending in: -age, -ment, -eur (agent nouns), -eau, -isme, -ier
Days, months, seasons, languages, most trees
le fromage, le gouvernement, le chapeau, le boulanger

blocks.text: Usually Feminine:

items: Nouns ending in: -tion, -sion, -ure, -ette, -elle, -ance, -ence, -té, -ière
Most fruits, academic subjects (except math? No, les mathématiques is feminine)
la nation, la voiture, la liberté, la boulangère

blocks.text: Tricky ones to memorize:

items: le problème (m) — ends in -e but masculine
la mer (f) — sea
le silence (m)
la page (f)

blocks.text: Strategy: Learn every noun with its article — un/le livre not just livre. This is standard practice.
Plural Formation
Add -s (usually silent) to most nouns:

items: un chat → des chats

blocks.text: Special cases:

items: -eau → -eaux: le chapeau → les chapeaux
-al → -aux: le cheval → les chevaux (horse)
-s, -x, -z: unchanged in plural

blocks.text: 🇷🇺 Russian has no articles at all. This is the single biggest structural difference. You will need to develop an intuition for when to use which article — this takes time. 🇬🇧 English has a/an (indefinite) and the (definite). French has those plus partitives.
Definite Articles (the)
le livre, la table, l'ami, l'heure, les enfants
Use definite articles:

items: For specific known things: Le chat de Marie (Marie's cat)
For general statements/categories: J'aime le café (I like coffee — in general)
With continents, countries, regions: la France, le Japon
With body parts (where English uses possessives): Je me lave les mains (I wash my hands)

blocks.text: Indefinite Articles (a/an, some)
un livre, une table, des enfants
Use for non-specific items: J'ai un chat (I have a cat — any cat).
After negation, un/une/des → de (d'): Je n'ai pas de chat.
Partitive Articles (some — uncountable)
These express an indefinite quantity of something uncountable:
Je mange du pain. (I eat [some] bread.) Elle boit de la bière. (She drinks [some] beer.) Il boit de l'eau. (He drinks [some] water.)
After negation: Je ne mange pas de pain.
🇷🇺 The partitive concept maps roughly onto the Russian genitive of quantity (выпить воды, съесть хлеба) — but in French it's obligatory with an article, not just a case ending.
Article Omission
Articles are dropped:

items: After être with professions (informal): Il est médecin. (He is a doctor.)
In lists: J'aime musique, sport, cuisine.
After en with feminine countries: en France, en Italie (but: au Japon)

blocks.text: Agreement
French adjectives agree with the noun in gender and number.
🇷🇺 Same principle as Russian adjectives. The mechanism is simpler in French (just -e for feminine, -s for plural, -es for feminine plural).
Note: beau/bel, nouveau/nouvel, vieux/vieil before masculine nouns starting with a vowel: un bel homme, un nouvel ami.
Position — BANGS Rule
Most French adjectives come after the noun. Only a small set of common adjectives come before:
BANGS — Beauty, Age, Number, Goodness/Badness, Size
Examples:

items: un grand homme — a great man (figuratively)
un homme grand — a tall man (physically)
une vieille voiture rouge — an old red car (vieille before, rouge after)

blocks.text: 🇷🇺 In Russian adjectives typically precede the noun. French adjective-after-noun is a key habit to build.
Subject Pronouns
🇷🇺 French has no pronoun-dropping (pro-drop). You must always state the subject — unlike Russian where говорю alone is fine without я.
On is extremely common in spoken French as informal "we": On va au cinéma = Nous allons au cinéma (We're going to the cinema).
Stress/Tonic Pronouns
Used after prepositions, for emphasis, after c'est:
moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles

items: C'est moi. — It's me.
Il parle avec elle. — He talks with her.
Moi, je préfère le café. — Me, I prefer coffee. (emphasis)

blocks.text: French verbs are grouped by their infinitive ending:
1st Group: -ER Verbs (parler — to speak)
Present tense:
The -e, -es, -e, -ent endings are silent. Only -ons and -ez are audibly distinct.
2nd Group: -IR Verbs (finir — to finish)
3rd Group: -RE Verbs (vendre — to sell)
These must be memorized. They are the most-used verbs in the language.
Être (to be)
Avoir (to have)
Aller (to go)
Faire (to do/make)
Other High-Priority Irregulars

rows: je peux, tu peux, il peut, ils peuvent
je veux, tu veux, il veut, ils veulent
je dois, tu dois, il doit, ils doivent
je sais, tu sais, il sait, ils savent
je vois, il voit, ils voient
je mets, il met, ils mettent

blocks.text: Présent — Present
Already covered above. Used for:

items: Current actions: Je mange. (I am eating / I eat)
Habitual actions: Je lis chaque matin. (I read every morning)
Near future (common): On part demain. (We're leaving tomorrow)

blocks.text: 🇬🇧 One French present = both "I eat" and "I am eating". No separate progressive.
Futur Proche — Near Future (going to)
Aller (present) + infinitive — extremely common in speech:
Je vais manger. — I'm going to eat. Il va pleuvoir. — It's going to rain. Nous allons partir. — We're going to leave.
Futur Simple — Simple Future (will)
Formed by adding future endings to the infinitive (-er/-ir verbs) or a special stem (-re verbs drop the -e):
parler → je parlerai, tu parleras, il parlera... finir → je finirai... vendre → je vendrai...
Irregular stems: être → ser-, avoir → aur-, aller → ir-, faire → fer-, venir → viendr-, voir → verr-, pouvoir → pourr-
Conditionnel — Conditional (would)
Same stem as futur simple + imperfect endings (-ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient):
je parlerais — I would speak il ferait — he would do
Used for: polite requests (Je voudrais...), hypothetical situations (Si j'avais...je ferais...).
Impératif — Imperative (commands)
Use the tu, nous, vous forms of present tense, dropping the subject pronoun. For -er verbs, drop the -s in tu form:

sections.title: Passé Composé vs Imparfait — The Core Tension

blocks.text: This is where most learners struggle. Both are "past tenses" but they do fundamentally different things.
Passé Composé — Completed Past Actions
Auxiliary (avoir or être) + past participle
Used for:

items: A completed action with a defined endpoint: J'ai mangé une pomme. (I ate an apple.)
A sequence of events: Il est entré, il a vu, il a conquis.
A sudden or punctual event in a narrative: Soudain, le téléphone a sonné.

blocks.text: Past participle formation:

items: -er verbs: parlé, mangé
-ir verbs: fini, parti
-re verbs: vendu, attendu
Irregulars: être → été, avoir → eu, faire → fait, voir → vu, prendre → pris, venir → venu

blocks.text: Imparfait — Ongoing/Habitual Past
Formed from the nous-present stem + endings: -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient
parler → nous parlons → je parlais, tu parlais, il parlait...
Used for:

items: Background descriptions: Il faisait beau. (The weather was nice.)
Ongoing states in the past: Elle était fatiguée. (She was tired.)
Habitual past actions: Quand j'étais enfant, je jouais au foot. (When I was a child, I used to play football.)
Interrupted actions: Je lisais quand il est arrivé. (I was reading when he arrived.)

blocks.text: 🇷🇺 This maps onto the Russian imperfective/perfective aspect distinction, though not perfectly. Passé composé ≈ perfective aspect (completed, result-focused). Imparfait ≈ imperfective aspect (process, state, habitual). A Russian-speaker's instinct here is often correct.
🇬🇧 English uses "I was doing" (progressive) for the imparfait background role, and "I did" (simple past) for the passé composé. French collapses this differently.
Contrast in context:
Hier, je lisais un livre quand mon ami a téléphoné. Yesterday, I was reading a book when my friend called. (lisais = ongoing background; a téléphoné = sudden completed event)
In passé composé, most verbs use avoir. About 17 common verbs use être — mostly verbs of motion and change of state. A mnemonic: DR. & MRS. VANDERTRAMP
+ all reflexive verbs use être.
Agreement rule: When using être, the past participle agrees with the subject like an adjective:
Elle est partie. (She left — feminine -e) Ils sont partis. (They left — masculine plural -s) Elles sont parties. (They [f] left — feminine plural -es)
With avoir, the past participle is invariable unless a direct object precedes the verb:
J'ai vu Marie. (no agreement) Je l'ai vue. (agreement because la/l' precedes — she was seen)
Standard negation wraps the verb with ne...pas:
Je ne parle pas. — I don't speak. Il ne mange pas. — He doesn't eat.
In compound tenses, ne...pas wraps the auxiliary:
Je n'ai pas mangé. — I didn't eat. Elle n'est pas venue. — She didn't come.
Other Negation Patterns

rows: Je ne bois ni café ni thé.

blocks.text: Spoken French: Dropping "ne"
In informal spoken French, ne is commonly dropped:
Je sais pas. (= Je ne sais pas) — I don't know. T'as pas vu mes clés ? — You haven't seen my keys? Y'a personne. (= Il n'y a personne) — There's no one.
🇷🇺 This is like colloquial Russian dropping formal particles. Written and formal French always keeps the ne.
Three main ways to ask questions in French:
1. Intonation (most common in speech)
Raise your voice at the end of a declarative sentence:
Tu parles français ? — You speak French? Il est là ? — Is he there?
2. Est-ce que (neutral, all registers)
Add est-ce que before the subject:
Est-ce que tu parles français ? Est-ce qu'il est là ?
3. Inversion (formal/written)
Invert subject and verb, hyphenated:
Parles-tu français ? Mangez-vous de la viande ?
With il/elle/on and a verb ending in a vowel, add -t-:
A-t-il faim ? (Is he hungry?) — a ends in vowel, so add -t-
Question Words
Où habitez-vous ? / Vous habitez où ? Pourquoi est-ce qu'il pleure ? Combien ça coûte ? Quel film tu veux regarder ?
Core Prepositions

rows: aller à Paris, être à la maison
par la fenêtre, deux fois par jour

blocks.text: Mandatory Contractions
When à or de precedes le or les, they must contract:
Je vais au marché. (à + le) Il parle aux enfants. (à + les) C'est le titre du livre. (de + le) Il vient des États-Unis. (de + les)
No contraction with la or l': à la gare, de l'eau.
Countries & Cities

items: Feminine countries (most ending in -e): en France, en Espagne, en Allemagne
Masculine countries: au Japon, au Canada, aux États-Unis
Cities: à Paris, à New York, à Rome

blocks.text: Direct Object Pronouns (replace the direct object)
Je vois Marie → Je la vois. Il mange le pain → Il le mange.
Indirect Object Pronouns (replace à + person)
Je parle à Marie → Je lui parle. Il donne le livre aux enfants → Il leur donne le livre.
Word Order
Object pronouns go before the verb (before the auxiliary in compound tenses):
Je le vois. — I see him. Je l'ai vu. — I saw him. Ne le mange pas ! — Don't eat it!
🇷🇺 Object pronouns in French are clitic — they must attach to the verb and cannot move freely. Russian allows freer word order for emphasis. In French, the position is fixed.
Double Object Pronouns
When both direct and indirect pronouns appear, order is:
me/te/nous/vous → le/la/les → lui/leur → y → en
Il me le donne. — He gives it to me. (me before le) Je le lui donne. — I give it to him. (le before lui)
Y and En
y = there / to it (replaces à + place/thing): Tu vas à Paris ? → Tu y vas ?
en = some of it / from there (replaces de + noun or a partitive): Tu veux du café ? → Tu en veux ? Il vient de Paris → Il en vient.

sections.title: Relative Pronouns: qui, que, dont, où

blocks.text: qui — who/which (subject of the relative clause)
L'homme qui parle est mon père. — The man who is speaking is my father. Le livre qui est sur la table est intéressant.
que (qu') — whom/which (direct object of the relative clause)
L'homme que je vois est mon père. — The man (whom) I see is my father. Le livre que tu lis est excellent.
Tip: qui is followed by a verb; que is followed by a subject + verb.
dont — of which/whose/about which (replaces *de + noun*)
Le livre dont je parle. — The book I'm talking about (about which I'm talking). L'homme dont je connais la femme. — The man whose wife I know.
où — where/when (replaces place or time reference)
La ville où j'habite. — The city where I live. Le jour où il est né. — The day when he was born.
The subjunctive (subjonctif) is a mood (not a tense) that expresses subjectivity — doubt, emotion, necessity, possibility, volition.
🇷🇺 Russian uses чтобы + past tense for many of the same contexts. The subjunctive in French is the equivalent mechanism — obligatory in its triggers.
When to Use It
The subjunctive follows specific trigger expressions. Key triggers:
Necessity / obligation:

items: il faut que — it is necessary that
il est nécessaire que

blocks.text: Volition / desire:

items: vouloir que — to want (someone to do)
préférer que, souhaiter que

blocks.text: Emotion:

items: être content/heureux/triste que
avoir peur que

blocks.text: Doubt / possibility:

items: douter que, ne pas croire que
il est possible/impossible que

blocks.text: After certain conjunctions:

items: bien que (although), pour que (so that), avant que (before), à moins que (unless), quoique (although)

blocks.text: Formation
From the ils-present stem + subjunctive endings: -e, -es, -e, -ions, -iez, -ent
parler → ils parlent → que je parle, que tu parles, qu'il parle, que nous parlions... finir → ils finissent → que je finisse... vendre → ils vendent → que je vende...
Irregular subjunctives:

items: être → que je sois, que tu sois, qu'il soit...
avoir → que j'aie, que tu aies, qu'il ait...
aller → que j'aille, que tu ailles, qu'il aille, que nous allions...
faire → que je fasse...
pouvoir → que je puisse...
vouloir → que je veuille...

blocks.text: In practice:
Je veux qu'il vienne. — I want him to come. Il faut que tu fasses tes devoirs. — You need to do your homework. Bien qu'il soit fatigué, il travaille. — Although he is tired, he works.
If the subject is the same in both clauses, use de + infinitive instead: Je veux venir. (not: Je veux que je vienne.)

sections.title: Tu vs Vous — Formality & Social Register

rows: friends, family, children, peers you know well, animals
strangers, people older than you, professional contexts, plural "you"

blocks.text: The social rules are real and violation causes genuine awkwardness:

items: Too much vous: Can feel cold or distancing among peers
Too much tu: Presumptuous or rude with strangers/elders

blocks.text: In France, the workplace is gradually shifting toward tu in many industries (tech, startups) — but traditional sectors (law, government, finance) still default to vous.
🇷🇺 Russian has the same distinction: ты/вы. Apply the same social logic — it maps very closely.
The gap between written/formal French and everyday spoken French is enormous — larger than in most European languages.
Key Informal Patterns
"C'est quoi ça ?" (What's that?) — informal for Qu'est-ce que c'est ?
"T'as fait quoi ?" — Informal for Qu'est-ce que tu as fait ? (What did you do?)
"On se casse ?" — Shall we leave? (Slang: se casser = to split/leave)
"Ouais" — Yeah (oui is formal "yes"; ouais is everyday)
"Bof" — A uniquely French non-committal expression: "meh / not really / I don't care"
"Quand même" — All the same / anyway / nonetheless — very common filler
"Voilà / Voilà voilà" — There you go / exactly / that's it — used constantly as a conversation filler/conclusion
Words that look like English but mean something different:
Savoir vs Connaître (both = "to know")

items: Savoir — to know a fact, how to do something:

blocks.text: Je sais qu'il vient. / Je sais nager.

items: Connaître — to know a person, place, or work:

blocks.text: Je connais Paris. / Tu connais Marie ?
Pouvoir vs Savoir (both can translate "can")

items: Pouvoir — to be able to (circumstances permit):

blocks.text: Je ne peux pas venir ce soir. (I can't come tonight — I'm busy)

items: Savoir — to know how to (skill):

blocks.text: Elle sait parler japonais. (She can speak Japanese — she knows how)
Partir vs Quitter vs Sortir (all = "to leave")

items: Partir — to leave (a place, intransitive): Il part demain.
Quitter — to leave (something/someone, transitive): Il quitte Paris. / Il quitte sa femme.
Sortir — to go out: On sort ce soir ?

blocks.text: Verb Tense Summary
Gender of Common Noun Endings (Pattern Guide)
Pronunciation Quick-Reference
Appendix: Study Priorities by Level
Beginner (months 1–3)

1. Pronunciation fundamentals — don't skip this 2. Present tense of être, avoir, aller, faire + regular -er verbs 3. Articles (definite/indefinite) — the most common error source 4. Numbers, days, months, time 5. Core vocabulary: 500-word pool 6. Basic questions with intonation and est-ce que
Intermediate (months 4–9)

1. Passé composé (avoir + être auxiliaries) 2. Imparfait — the PC/Imparfait distinction 3. Object pronouns (y, en, le/la/les, lui/leur) 4. Futur simple and conditionnel 5. Negation variants 6. Core vocabulary: 1000–1500 word pool
Upper-Intermediate (months 10–18)

1. Subjunctive (high-frequency triggers) 2. Relative pronouns (dont especially) 3. Reflexive verbs fully 4. Register differences — spoken vs written 5. Idioms and filler expressions 6. Vocabulary: 2000+ words
Happy learning — Bon courage ! 🇫🇷
